coredata.dc:title: Rapid synthesis of highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via a microreactor with foamy copper for the detection of Hg2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yong
Rao, Longshi
Li, Zongtao
Lu, Hanguang
Yan, Caiman
Yu, Shudong
Ding, Xinrui
Yu, Binhai
coredata.dc:description: A rapid synthetic method for highly photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was developed by using a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities. By taking advantage of the foamy copper, we obtained a high quantum yield (QY) of 84.1%, which was more efficient than most of the previous reports. Compared to the procedure without adding foamy copper, the QY when foamy copper was used was two times higher. The formation mechanism of the N-CQDs, which involved molecular condensation and the formation of carbon cores, was demonstrated. To explore the relationship between the use of a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities and the PL properties of the N-CQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was employed. The results show that the surface functional groups and elemental contents influence the PL emission. We further demonstrate the application of the N-CQDs as probes for heavy metal ion detection. The results indicated that N-CQDs are very suitable for use as nanoprobes for Hg2+ ion detection and that the lowest detection limit (LOD) for Hg2+ is 2.104nM, which is superior to many other research studies. This method highlights that the value of microreactor synthetic system as a tool for the environmental and hazard detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Microreactor
Foamy copper
Ions detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The formation mechanism of N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having 98% porosity.
(a) Schematic diagram of the microreactor. (b) Enlarged view of the microchannel reaction region with foamy copper.
The QYs of N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having 98% porosity at different temperatures.

The QYs of N-CQDs synthesized under different reaction condition: the red line represents the reaction region with foamy copper having 98% porosity, and the blue line represents the reaction region without foamy copper. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) The QY and (b) FWHM of PL spectra of N-CQDs synthesized by microreactors with foamy copper having different porosities at different reaction temperatures (150–230°C).
(a) Normalized PL spectra and (b) PL peak position of the N-CQDs synthesized by microreactors with foamy copper having different porosities.

TEM images of the N-CQDs synthesized by the a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities: (a) 98%, (b) 90%, (c) 80%, (d) 70%, (e) 60%, and (f) 50%. Inset images are the size distribution histograms.
XRD patterns of the N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities.
FTIR spectra of the N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foaming copper having different porosities.
Full-scan XPS spectra of the N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having 98%, 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, and 50% porosities.
C1s spectra of N-CQDs: (a)-(f) represent the N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having porosities of 98%, 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, and 50%, respectively.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra of N-CQDs and (b) the excitation-dependent PL spectra of the N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having a 98% porosity.
Stability of the N-CQDs at different (a) pH values and (b) NaCl concentrations.
(a) Comparison of PL intensities of the N-CQDs after the addition of different metal ions. (b) The PL intensity of N-CQDs after the addition of different metal ions under 365nm irradiation.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of solutions of N-CQDs upon adding Hg2+ solution (from top to bottom: 0–70μM). (b) Plots of the fluorescence intensity at 504nm versus the concentration of Hg2+. The inset shows the linear relationship of F0/F versus the concentrations of Hg2+ ions in the range of 0-0.1μM. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
Comparison of different synthetic methods for N-CQDs.

row.entry:
Citric acid and ethylenediamine,
Citric acid and l-cysteine,
Sodium citrate and NH4HCO3

Citric acid and dicyandiamide,
Uric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The atomic percentages and corresponding C1s, N1s and O1s peaks of the centre binding energies of different N-CQDs.
XPS data analysis of the C1s spectra of six types of NCQDs.
Comparison of the sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Rapid synthesis of highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via a microreactor with foamy copper for the detection of Hg2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Centre of Green Manufacturing for Energy-Saving and New-Energy Technology, School of Mechanical and Automotive Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Centre of Green Manufacturing for Energy-Saving and New-Energy Technology
School of Mechanical and Automotive Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of California, Berkeley, CA 94720, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid synthetic method for highly photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was developed by using a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities. By taking advantage of the foamy copper, we obtained a high quantum yield (QY) of 84.1%, which was more efficient than most of the previous reports. Compared to the procedure without adding foamy copper, the QY when foamy copper was used was two times higher. The formation mechanism of the N-CQDs, which involved molecular condensation and the formation of carbon cores, was demonstrated. To explore the relationship between the use of a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities and the PL properties of the N-CQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was employed. The results show that the surface functional groups and elemental contents influence the PL emission. We further demonstrate the application of the N-CQDs as probes for heavy metal ion detection. The results indicated that N-CQDs are very suitable for use as nanoprobes for Hg2+ ion detection and that the lowest detection limit (LOD) for Hg2+ is 2.104nM, which is superior to many other research studies. This method highlights that the value of microreactor synthetic system as a tool for the environmental and hazard detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered great interest because of their unique and fascinating chemical, electronic and optical properties, including excellent water solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and high stability [1–4]. These remarkable properties distinguish them from traditional fluorescent materials and make them promising candidates to replace heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) for numerous exciting applications, such as in photocatalysis [5], sensing [6], bioimaging [7], energy conversion and photovoltaic devices [8]. Although many successes have been achieved, the properties of these CQDs in terms of their quantum yield (QY) greatly restrict their further practical application. To improve the QY, many strategies for the preparation of CQDs have been explored, such as doping heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen, phosphorus and sulfur) into the CQDs. Among these heteroatoms, nitrogen-doping has been observed to be a facile and effective method to improve the QY of CQDs. Zheng et al. reported the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a QY up to 88.6% through a facile thermal pyrolysis route [9]. Tan et al. developed a method for the large-scale synthesis of N-CQDs with a QY of 83% using isophorone diisocyanate as a single carbon source under microwave irradiation [10]. Zhu et al. produced N-CQDs with a QY up to 80.6% from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) via a hydrothermal method [11]. Ding et al. have developed a simple and solvent-free synthetic strategy to fabricate N-CQDs from CA and dicyandiamide with a strong yellowish-green fluorescence emission and a high QY of 73.2% [12]. Dong et al. prepared N-CQDs with a high QY of 73% using a method conducted at 200°C for 3h [13]. However, because of the lack of intensive theoretical studies and sophisticated technical guidance, the rapid, large-scale preparation of specific CQDs with targeted luminescent properties has been a tedious and time-consuming task. Therefore, developing a method for the rapid and large-scale production of CQDs as well as obtaining high QYs, is very attractive.

The mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most dangerous and ubiquitous pollutants and exhibits bioaccumulation and high toxicity; thus, it poses serious threats to the environment and health, including damage to the ecosystem, the heart, the nervous system and the brain. Therefore, rapid, sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in environmental and biological samples is very significant. Fluorescence analytical methods have attracted a great deal of attention because of their advantages of high sensitivity, easy operation and non-destructiveness. CQDs have been used for the detection of Hg2+ due to their particular chemical and optical properties [14–17]. It is well known that highly photoluminescent (PL) CQDs with high stability are crucial for ion detection. However, the CQDs synthesized by common methods did not achieve high QY values, thereby making their practical application difficult. Therefore, a facile and inexpensive technique to synthesize highly PL CQDs is highly desirable.

Recently, microreactors have drawn our attention because of their advantages with regards to their quick heat and mass transfer properties, controllable feed rates, high-speed mixing and great reproducibility [18–20]. These outstanding properties have allowed their widespread application to organic synthesis, nanoparticle synthesis and pharmaceutical production. Moreover, microreactors have been regarded as a preferred method for the synthesis of high quality metal-oxides and semiconductor QDs [21–23]. Tian et al. developed a novel method using a microreactor to synthesize full-colour emitting CdSe semiconductor nanocrystals with good monodispersity [24]. Yang et al. synthesized the green-emitting ZnO QDs with QYs of 64.7% using an ultrasonic microreactor [25]. However, among the many reports on the use of microreactors for the synthesis of QDs, most of the microreactor productivities are inefficient and hardly obtain products with high QYs. Moreover, there have been few reports on the synthesis of CQDs using microreactors. With these facts in mind, we focused on developing a microreactor for the rapid and large-scale synthesis of N-CQDs with a high QY.

In this work, we developed a method for the rapid synthesis of N-CQDs with a high QY using a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities. By utilizing the microreactor, we can rapidly synthesize N-CQDs on a large scale in less than 8min. Moreover, using foamy copper allowed us to obtain stable N-CQDs with a high QY of 84.1%, which is a new record for QDs synthesized using a microreactor. The relationship between the microreactor with the use of foamy copper having different porosities and the PL properties of the N-CQDs was also investigated. The as-prepared N-CQDs were used to detect Hg2+ ions, and the lowest detection limit (LOD) was 2.104nM, which is superior to those achieved in many other research studies. This study fully highlights the value of the microreactor synthetic system as a tool in the synthesis of N-CQDs for ion detection.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), HgCl2, NiCl2, FeCl2, BaCl2, NaCl, MnCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, CrCl3, PbCl2, CuCl2, HCl, NaOH, quinine sulfate and pH-buffer purchased from Aladdin were used without further purification. All reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade. Distilled water was used for all experiments.

Highly photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were successfully synthesized via a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities. A schematic diagram of the microreactor is shown in Fig. 1 (a). When foamy copper with different porosities is used, the most important reaction region is the microchannels (Fig. 1(b)), which can realize transfer of a liquid state to a gas state, a property that is beneficial for the nucleation and growth of quantum dots (QDs). The structures of foamy copper with different porosities (50–98%) were characterized by field emission scanning election microscopy (FE-SEM), as presented in Fig. S1. The typical steps of the experiment are as follows: first, a colourless transparent solution was prepared by dissolving CA (4.5g) in 120ml of deionized water, followed by a few minutes of continuous stirring at room temperature. Then, EDA (3.0ml) was slowly dropped into the CA solution with vigorous stirring to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. Before the mixtures were injected into the microreactor, the microreactor was preheated to the set temperature, and distilled water was injected to remove impurities and to evaluate the air impermeability of the system. Subsequently, when the set temperature was reached, the mixtures were injected by a pump with a flowmeter through a stainless steel tube into the microreactor. The microreactor temperature (real temperature between 150°C and 230°C) was precisely controlled by a digital temperature controller. Importantly, to ensure that the flow rate stabilized, a check valve was placed between the flowmeter and microreactor to prevent any solution and vapor from reverse flowing when the microreactor was operating at high temperatures. The products were collected by in a flask and soaked in ice water. Before testing, the products were transferred into dialysis bag (MWco 1000) and then soaked in beaker with deionized water for 24h to remove impurities.

For the detection of metal ions, various metal ion solutions, including those containing Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Na+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Pb2+ and Cu2+, were used to examine the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs. The aforementioned ion solutions were prepared from the corresponding chloride (Cl−) reagents at a concentration of 50μM and pH of 7 (pH-buffer). Next, 0.2ml of the ion solution was added into 3ml of the N-CQDs and allowed to react for 1min under light stirring to form a homogeneous solution. To evaluate the sensitivity of the N-CQDs towards Hg2+, a series of concentrations (ranging from 0 to 70μM) of Hg2+ were added into the aqueous solution containing the same concentration of N-CQDs. The PL spectra of the N-CQDs in the absence and presence of various metal ions were measured with a 365nm excitation source. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

The structure of foamy copper was measured by FE-SEM (Merlin, Germany). The surface morphology of the N-CQDs was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples were prepared by dropping diluted nanocrystal colloidal suspensions onto carbon-coated 200mesh copper grids. The phase purity and structure of the as-prepared N-CQDs were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8-Advance, Bruker, Germany) with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ=0.15418nm) at a counting rate of 0.02° per minute in a scanning angle (2θ) range from 5° to 80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK) with a mono Al-Kα excitation source (1486.6eV). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded from 4000 to 400cm−1 on an FTIR spectrometer (VERTEX 33, Bruker, Germany) with KBr discs after vacuum-drying for 12h. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrometer (UV–vis, Shimadzu, Japan) over an excitation range from 350nm to 700nm at 1nm intervals. The excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan) with a Xe lamp as the excitation source. The quantum yield (QY) of the N-CQDs was calculated using the following equation [26].
(1)
Q =
Q
s t
K x
K
s t
η x
η
s t
where the Q is the QY and the subscripts X and ST denote test and standard, respectively; quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY 0.54 at 365nm) was used as the standard. K represents the slope determined from the ratio of the linear curves of the integrated fluorescence intensity to the UV–vis absorbance. Ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent. The integrated emission intensity is the area under the photoluminescence curve in the wavelength range of 350–750nm. Graphs were obtained by plotting the integrated emission intensity against the absorbance. Meanwhile, the standards were chosen to ensure that they absorbed at the excitation wavelength of choice for the test samples. To reduce reabsorption effects, the absorbances of the standard and test samples at and above the excitation wavelength did not exceed 0.1.

In our previous study, we have screened many conditions and showed that the reaction temperature has a significant effect on the QY. To illuminate the effect of the reaction temperature on the synthesis of N-CQDs, the experiment using foamy copper with 98% porosity embedded in a microchannel was conducted at different reaction temperatures. The QY of the as-prepared N-CQDs was calculated using Eq. (1) and quinine sulfate as a standard (QY=54%), as shown in Fig. 2 . As the reaction temperature increased, the QY of the N-CQDs first increased and then decreased, reaching a peak of 84.1% at 190°C, which is higher than the QY values previously reported for N-CQDs. We found that the formation of N-CQDs mainly involves two processes: condensation and carbonization, as shown in Scheme 1 . The small-molecule fluorophores and carbon core were generated during the molecular condensation and carbonization processes, respectively. The reaction temperature influences the extent of condensation and carbonization. At low temperatures (T≤190°C), the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CA and the amino groups of EDA undergo complex condensation and carbonization reactions, involving intermolecular condensation between the CA molecules themselves and between CA and EDA molecules to form the small-molecule fluorophores, Then, these fluorophores assembled to form a carbon core [11,27–29]. It is believed that the QY of N-CQDs depend on the number of molecular fluorophores, and more molecular fluorophores lead to a higher QY [30]. Before reaching the highest value, the QY of the N-CQDs increases due to the continuous formation of small-molecule fluorophores and the growth of these small-molecule fluorophores into large-molecule fluorophores. With the further increase in temperature, the large-molecule fluorophores are gradually carbonized to form carbon core-containing N-CQDs. At high temperature (190°C<T), the carbon core grows gradually, and the QY decreases due to the decrease in molecular fluorophores because of the slower extension and formation of molecular fluorophores compared with their consumption. This process is a chemical equilibrium between the starting materials, molecular fluorophores and carbon core. In other words, there is a competition between molecular fluorophore formation on the surface of the N-CQDs and carbon core growth, leading to a change in the number of molecular fluorophores. Therefore, a proper reaction temperature exists for the N-CQDs with the highest QY.

Under the optimum reaction conditions, the total reaction times are not more than 8min (120ml of solution). These reactions are much faster than most of those previously reported on the same scale, as shown in Table 1 . In addition, this method not only has obvious merits in terms of efficiency but also remarkable merits in scale. All of these results indicate that microreactors are advantageous for high reaction rates, larger scales and high QYs.

Compared to the reaction conducted without added foamy copper, the reaction region with foamy copper having 98% porosity has an advantage regarding the QY, as presented in Fig. 3 . The reaction temperature is 190°C. Interestingly, the QY when foamy copper was used is two times higher than that when foamy copper was not used. This result indicated that the use of the microreactor together with foamy copper improved the QY due to the advantages of foamy copper with regards to its quick heat and mass transfer properties, high-speed and homogeneous mixing. The temperature in the microreactor was higher than the boiling point of the mixture solution, and thus, when the mixture was injected, the solution moved from the liquid state to the gas state, which will affect small-molecule fluorophore formation and carbon core generation. When the foamy copper was added, it blocks the solution or gas movement and creates a high pressure atmosphere. This pressure is believed to be beneficial to molecular fluorophore and carbon core generation. The pressure increase with increasing reaction temperature creates an enormous pressure difference between the inlet and outlet, and an intermittent outflow phenomenon was observed. Due to the quick heat transfer and rapid mixing properties of foamy copper, molecular fluorophores can grow adequately, a large number of molecular fluorophores can be generated in the foamy copper cell. Of course, too high of a reaction temperature will lead to serious carbonation and a decrease in the QY. For the channel without foamy copper, there is no high pressure environment, and the residence time was reduced; thus, only a few molecular fluorophores were formed. Therefore, we believe the difference of the QY between the channel with foamy copper and the channel without foamy copper in the microreactor is due to the different concentrations of molecular fluorophores.

The porosities of foamy copper impact its heat transfer and mixing performance. Therefore, the effects of foamy copper with different porosities at different temperatures on the QYs of the as-prepared N-CQDs were investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 (a)–(b). Fig. 4(a) shows that as the reaction temperature increased, all of the QYs of the N-CQDs first increased and then decreased, reaching the highest QY at 190°C. This indicates that the optimal reaction temperature for the N-CQDs is 190°C. Meanwhile, when the porosities of foamy copper decreased at the same temperature, the QY of the N-CQDs decreased, especially for the N-CQDs synthesized using foamy copper with 98% porosity. The decline in the porosities of foamy copper means that the residence time increased and the productivity was reduced. In our experiment, the size of foamy copper was 67×40×2mm, and the scale of the holes in the foamy copper ranged from 0.8mm to 0.3mm. As the porosity dropped by 10%, the size of the hole decreased almost two-fold. A small porosity leads to the formation of steam, which is hard to diffuse, when the reaction temperature reaches a certain level, which induced serious carbonization of the N-CQDs while decreasing the nitrogen-modifying function. Along with the reaction processing, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL spectra of the N-CQDs increased as the porosities decreased, which may be related to the fact that a smaller porosity prolongs the residence time and is good for carbon core growth, as shown in Fig. 4(b). As the reaction temperature increased, the FWHM first decreased and then increased, which is different from the variation trend of the QY. The FWHM ranged from 84.12 to 80.7nm at reaction temperatures from 150 to 230°C, showing that the FWHM of the N-CQDs is wide compared to that of the other semiconductor QDs. This is one of the main differences between N-CQDs and QDs. In addition, the minimum FWHM was obtained when the reaction temperature was 190°C, which in this case is the same as the QY.

In addition, the PL spectra of the N-CQDs were also investigated to further demonstrate the effects of different porosities of foamy copper on the properties of the N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 (a)–(b). When the porosities of foamy copper decreased, the PL peak position ranged from 502 to 521nm (Fig. 5(b)), which is ascribed to the reduction in the porosity of foamy copper resulting in the crystal carbon core growing larger. These results are consistent with the above analysis, namely, that decreases in the porosity of foamy copper impacted the PL spectra.

The effect of foamy copper having different porosities on the structures of N-CQDs is also investigated by a TEM. The TEM images in Fig. 6 (a)–(f) show that the N-CQD samples possess a nearly spherical shape with average diameters of 2.47nm, 2.75nm, 2.83nm, 3.03nm, 3.21nm and 3.34nm, respectively, indicating that they have a narrow size distribution. As the porosity of foamy copper decreases, the average diameter of the N-CQDs increases, and some large crystals appeared. This phenomenon may be related to the prolonged growth stage of the crystal carbon core and its ability to grow adequately due to the increase in the hole density per unit volume when the porosity of foamy copper decreases. Meanwhile, the size distribution became increasingly narrower as the porosity of foamy copper decreased because of the more uniform heat transfer. In addition, the TEM images show that all of the samples do not have remarkable lattice fringes, revealing that they are amorphous structures. Consistent with PL analysis, these results indicate that foamy copper with a smaller porosity is beneficial to carbon core growth.

The XRD patterns of the N-CQDs synthesized using a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities at a reaction temperature of 190°C are shown in Fig. 7 . The patterns of the N-CQDs prepared using foamy copper with porosities of 98%, 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, and 50% have a similar and broad single diffraction peak at 23.79°, 23.64°, 23.65°, 23.33°, 23.26°, and 22.83°, respectively. As the porosity of foamy copper decreased, the diffraction peak of the corresponding N-CQDs slightly blueshifted, which may be related to the crystal lattice growth direction. The XRD patterns also display the highly disordered amorphous carbon of the N-CQDs. Meanwhile, no impurity peaks were observed, confirming the high purity of the three products. In addition, the relative intensity of the XRD diffraction peak increased, and the peak width at half height decreased as the porosities of foamy copper decreased, which further corroborated that a smaller porosity of foamy copper was advantageous for crystal growth. All of the TEM and XRD results are consistent, supporting that the N-CQDs are amorphous nanoparticles without discernible lattice structures.

However, using only the characterization techniques described above, we still cannot understand the differences in the molecular structures of the N-CQDs synthesized by a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities. Therefore, to further explore the possible causes, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out to explore the chemical composition and chemical bonding of the N-CQDs.

The surface functional groups of the as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 8 . The FTIR spectra of the N-CQDs synthesized via a microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities are similar, except for the relative intensities of certain bands that vary and indicate unique characteristics. In the FTIR spectra of the N-CQDs, the absorption bands at 3400–3150cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, which indicates the formation of −OH during the synthesis of the six types of NCQDs. The absorption peak located at approximately 2933cm−1 signifies the stretching vibrations of −CH3. In addition, the intense peak centred at 1665cm−1 is ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of CO. The stretching vibrations bands of CC (1555cm−1), CN=(1445cm−1), and CO (1285cm−1) are observed for each of the NCQD samples, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures in the six types of NCQDs during the synthesis [31]. A NH bending vibration at 740cm−1 is also observed. Previous research showed that the band at approximately 1250–1650cm−1 could be ascribed to the skeletal vibrations of the aromatic rings. All of these functional groups contribute to the excellent solubility of NCQDs. By carefully comparing the FTIR spectra of different N-CQDs, we can make several important observations. On the one hand, the centre of the broad absorption peak of the typical stretching vibrations of OH/NH bonds shifts from 3400 to 3150cm−1 when the porosity of the foamy copper used to synthesize the N-CQDs is changed from 98% to 50%. This result relates to amidogen modification on the surface of the N-CQDs. On the other hand, the −CH3 vibration bands at approximately 2933cm−1 become increasingly obvious as the foamy copper porosities decreased. The final and most important observation is the complex absorption peak in the fingerprint region (1000–400cm−1) that includes CO, CH, NH and OH bands; differences existing in this region of the FTIR spectra of the different N-CQDs structures were hardly perceptible. Thus, it is necessary to use other characterization techniques to further explore the mechanism of formation. According to the analysis, we can draw the conclusion that the contents of the surface functional groups and elements lead to different PL intensities between the six types of N-CQDs.

The XPS technique was performed to further explore the properties of the functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10 . The full-scan XPS spectra of the N-CQDs shown in Fig. 9 indicate that all of the N-CQDs consist of carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O). Furthermore, the atomic percentages from the corresponding C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of the six different N-CQDs are depicted in Table 2 . These data clearly demonstrate that N atoms have been doped into the framework of N-CQDs. Table 2 reveals that as the porosity of foamy copper decreases, the carbon content increases, whereas the nitrogen and oxygen contents decrease. This suggests that the increase in hole intensity will promote carbon core formation. This analysis is consistent with the TEM and XRD results.

All of the high-resolution N-CQDs spectra contain a C1s band that can be divided into three peaks centred at 284.5–284.6, 285.6–286.1 and 287.5–287.8eV corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO/CN, respectively, as shown in Fig. 10(a)–(f). Meanwhile, the high-resolution N1s spectra of the NCQDs are shown in Fig. S2 (a)-(f) and can be decomposed into two peaks at approximately 400–400.1 and 401.2–402.0eV, which are attributed to the CN and NH groups, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectra of NCQDs are shown in Fig. S3(a)–(f), in which the deconvoluted peaks at approximately 531.8-532.1 and 533.8-534.1eV can be assigned to the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively. All six types of NCQDs contain similar O-containing functional groups, which enhance the solubility of the NCQDs in water. The XPS data analysis of the C1s spectra of the six types of N-CQDs are presented in Table 3 . The C1s spectra show that the relative content of the XPS peak at 284.6eV gradually increases as foamy copper porosity decreases from 98% to 50%, revealing that the degree of carbonization and the content of carbonyl groups of the N-CQDs increase, which is consistent with the FTIR results. Meanwhile, the relative contents of the XPS peaks at 286eV and 287.7eV gradually decrease, indicating a corresponding decrease in the contents of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The above XPS spectroscopic analysis agrees well with the FTIR results, further verifying the existence of surface groups on the N-CQDs and the relationship between the microreactor with foamy copper having different porosities and product. Moreover, Table 2 reveals that smaller porosities of foamy copper improve the degree of carbonization and that higher N elemental contents are helpful for generating a high PL intensity. The FTIR and XPS analysis demonstrates that the six different N-CQDs contain abundant surface functional groups (CC, CO, NH, −OH, etc.), suggesting unexceptional water solubility without the need for further passivation. Thus, these N-CQDs have great potential applications in bioscience and hazard detection.

The reaction conditions can be screened quickly and effectively by using the microreactor system. The optimized conditions for the reaction temperature and porosities of foamy copper are 190°C and 98%, respectively. Fig. 11 (a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum and the excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs obtained under the optimized conditions. The inset images show the bright colour of the solution under sunlight radiation and under UV lamp radiation, indicating the formation of fluorescent CQDs. The N-CQDs show strong absorption at approximately 350nm with a tail extending into the visible range. The absorption peak located at 350nm represents the typical absorption of an aromatic π system (n-π* transition) or other connected surface functional groups. The surface functional groups including oxygen functional groups and nitrogen functional groups usually readily attach on the surface of N-CQDs during the synthetic process; these groups play an important role in generating the fluorescence under UV-light illumination at a specific wavelength. The excitation and emission peaks are located at 430 and 502nm, respectively, which is different from our previous results. Moreover, the emission peak position of the N-CQDs depends on the excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. 11(b). The PL spectra exhibit a strong emission peak at 510nm with an excitation of 430nm. Interestingly, the observed excitation-dependent emission luminescence properties are similar to those reported previously. The maximum emission redshifts with increasing excitation wavelength. This phenomenon may be related to the surface state, molecular state or other solvation effects.

The influences of pH and high ionic strength environments are also essential to the stability of the N-CQDs, which are usually used for bioimaging and analytical detection. Therefore, the stability of the N-CQDs was investigated at different pH values and ionic strengths, as shown in Fig. 12 (a)–(b). The PL spectra of the N-CQDs were recorded at different pH values by adjusting the pH with 0.1M solutions of HCl and NaOH. Increasing the pH value from 1.0 to 7.0 led to a sharp change in the PL intensity, but a further increase from 8.0 to 13.0 resulted in a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs strongly depended on the pH. The largest response was at a pH of 7, which is suitable for applications involving in vivo environments. In addition, at different NaCl concentrations (Fig. 12(b)), the PL intensities show no remarkable changes, which indicated that the N-CQDs were stable even under high ionic strength conditions. The stability of the N-CQDs under high-salt conditions ensured their feasibility in more complex environments. These results show that the N-CQDs are suitable for bioscience studies conducted at various physical salt concentrations. Besides, the stability of the N-CQDs was investigated under different ultraviolet lamp exposure times and storage for 5 months under ambient conditions, respectively, as shown in Fig. S4. The N-CQDs were exposed under the ultraviolet lamp (10W) for 21h. After 21h, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs decreased 3.56%. This result indicates that N-CQDs possess good photo-stability, which may be benefit from the carbon core-based PL centre. Moreover, when the N-CQDs were stored for 5 months under ambient conditions, the PL intensity decreased 15.9%. These stability data signify that the N-CQDs have an immense potential application value.

To examine the properties of the N-CQDs for hazard detection, we explored the feasibility of the N-CQDs as nanoprobes for the detection of representative metal ions, as shown in Fig. 13 . The fluorescence responses of the N-CQDs were studied in the presence of different metal ions (i.e., Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Na+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, and Cu2+) at concentrations of 50μM. Notably, the presence of other individual ions results in almost no apparent quenching of the fluorescence except for Hg2+, implying that the addition of Hg2+ could induce the quenching of the fluorescence emission of the N-CQDs in aqueous solution. Fig. 13(a) indicated that compared to the other ions, Hg2+ induced a great extent of fluorescence quenching, and the quenching efficiency was greater than 50%, indicating good selectivity for Hg2+. Thus, N-CQDscould be used as a nanosensing platform for Hg2+ ion detection. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs by Hg2+ may be related to the interaction between the ions and the carboxylate or hydroxyl groups, which promotes the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process, resulting in a substantial decrease in the fluorescence of the NCQDs. The excited-state electron transfer to the LUMO of the Hg2+ ion from the N-CQDs followed by the electrons returning to the ground state of the N-CQDs in a non-radiative transfer leads to a quenching of the fluorescence of the N-CQDs [32–35]. As shown in Fig. 13(b), all of the other samples exhibited bright blue fluorescence except for the sample with Hg2+, thereby clearly demonstrating that the added Hg2+ ions resulted in PL quenching of the N-CQDs under irradiation from a UV lamp, which was consistent with the results of the fluorescence spectral analysis. These results clearly demonstrated that the N-CQDs could function as nanoprobes for the detection of Hg2+ ions.

Based on the results described above, we evaluated the ability of this analytical method to quantitatively detect Hg2+. There was a phenomenon of Hg2+ concentration-dependent quenching of the fluorescence of the N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 14 (a), the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs at approximately 504nm gradually declines with increasing Hg2+ concentration in a range from 0 to 70μM, suggesting that this system is sensitive to Hg2+ ions. The relative PL intensity follows the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
/ F
= 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs at 504nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ion solution, respectively. The variable Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of Hg2+. A good linear correlation (R2 =0.9903) was observed over the concentration range of 0-0.1μM (Fig. 14(b) inset). The LOD was calculated to be 2.104nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 based on the standard deviation (SD) of 10 blank measurements and the slope (Ksv) of the calibration curve at levels approximating the LOD according to the formula LOD=3.0 (SD/Ksv) [36]. This detection sensitivity of Hg2+ is superior to that of some previously reported examples (Table 4 ) and satisfies the sensitivity requirement for the detection of Hg2+ in drinking water of 10nM required by the US Environmental Protection Agency. The above analysis clearly confirmed that N-CQDs can be used as a highly efficient nanoprobe for Hg2+ detection.

In this paper, we proposed a highly efficient method for the synthesis of N-CQDs with a high QY by using a microreactors with foamy copper having different porosities. By taking advantage of the microreactor, we achieved a high QY of 84.1% when using a microreactor with foamy copper having a 98% porosity, and this yield was greater than that obtained using the common hydrothermal approach. The formation mechanism of the N-CQDs was investigated, and the small-molecule fluorophores and carbon core were shown to be generated during the condensation and carbonization processes, respectively. Moreover, FITR and XPS analysis was carried out, and the results show that the surface functional groups and the elemental content influence the PL emission, especially the nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups. The PL stability of the N-CQDs was also investigated at different pH values and NaCl concentrations, revealing that these N-CQDs have good stability. Additional experiments demonstrated that NCQDs are very suitable for use as nanoprobes for the detection of Hg2+ ions. It is believed that this strategy offers a new method for developing low-cost, highly sensitive and highly selective sensors for environmental and biological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1401249, 51405161 and 51375177), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2014A30312017), and the Science & Technology Program of Guangdong Province (2015B010132002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.140.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yong Tang received the MS degree in 1989 in the Institute of mechanical engineering, Anhui University and Ph.D degree in 1994 in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. Now, he is a professor of South China University of Technology, and his current research is the photodevice and development of application technology.

Longshi Rao received the BE degree in School of mechanical and automotive engineering, East China JiaoTong University in 2014. He is currently working toward the Ph.D degree in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. His research interests include the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications.

Zongtao Li received the BE degree in 2008 and Ph.D degree in 2013 in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. Now, he is an associate professor of South China University of Technology, and his current research is the photodevice and development of application technology.

Hanguang Lu is an undergraduate student in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. His research interests include the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications.

Caiman Yan is an undergraduate student in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. His research interests include the synthesis of carbon dots and their applications in light emitting diodes.

Shudong Yu received the BE degree in School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology in 2013. He is currently working toward the Ph.D degree in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. His research interests include the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications.

Xinrui Ding received his BE degree in 2010 and Ph.D degree in 2015 in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. Now, he is a postdoctor of University of California Berkeley. His research interests include the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications.

Binhai Yu received his Ph.D degree in the School of mechanical engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology. Now, he is senior researcher in the School of mechanical and automotive engineering, South China University of Technology. His research interests include photodevice and development of application technology.
